title: Why Do Some Promises Stick and Others Don't?

challenges.stem: A startup founder tells investors 'I'm fully committed to this company.' Six months later they take a side consulting gig. The investors are upset. What would have made the original commitment credible?

options.text: A structural device: vesting schedule with cliff, personal financial investment, or a non-compete — something that makes side work costly
A stronger verbal promise with more emotional conviction
Investors should have judged the founder's character better before investing
A written contract stating the founder must work full-time

challenges.explanation: A written contract (option D) is the closest, but it's only credible if enforcement is likely and penalties are costly. The best commitment devices make breaking the commitment structurally expensive: vesting cliffs mean leaving costs equity, personal investment means failure costs personal wealth, non-competes mean leaving costs future options. Verbal promises and character judgments are cheap — they cost nothing to break.

challenges.stem: Odysseus tied himself to the mast before hearing the Sirens' song. He didn't trust his future self. What psychological force was he defending against?

options.text: Fear — specifically, the fear that temporal discounting would make his future self override his present self's rational decision
Identity — he was protecting his identity as a wise leader from the Sirens' identity-dissolving song
Incentives — the Sirens offered something so rewarding that his incentive structure would flip in the moment
Uncertainty — he couldn't predict how his future self would behave under the Sirens' influence

challenges.explanation: The Sirens don't offer a rational incentive — they dissolve who you are. Odysseus's commitment device protects his identity as someone who completes the journey. In the moment of the song, he becomes someone else entirely — someone who wants to crash the ship. The device isn't about managing incentives or uncertainty; it's about binding your present identity so it persists through experiences that would transform it.

pairs.back: A deliberate destruction of future options that makes a promise credible by making it structurally impossible (or prohibitively expensive) to break
External commitment device — destroying retreat to make soldiers fight with total commitment AND signal to enemies that you can't be waited out
Internal commitment device — binding your future self against temptations your present self can foresee but your future self won't resist
The tendency to overvalue present rewards and undervalue future costs — the psychological flaw that commitment devices are designed to counteract

pairs.left: Putting $1000 in a savings account with early withdrawal penalty
pairs.right: Commitment device against spending: making access to the money costly

pairs.left: Announcing your goal publicly on social media
pairs.right: Social commitment device: making failure embarrassing (moderate strength)

pairs.left: Saying 'I really want to exercise more this year'
pairs.right: Not a commitment device — a wish with zero structural constraint
High-stakes commitment device: making departure catastrophically expensive

pairs.left: Vendor requiring 50% deposit before starting work
pairs.right: Commitment device ensuring client doesn't walk away mid-project

before.label: Relying on willpower
before.text: I really mean it this time. I'll be disciplined. I'll follow through. I just need more motivation and a better mindset.

after.label: Designing structural commitment
after.text: Motivation fluctuates. Structure doesn't. I'll make breaking this commitment more expensive than keeping it — publicly, financially, or socially. My future self won't have the option to defect, regardless of how it feels in the moment.
